risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The random numbers for group assignment were generated by the research assistant (not the counselors) of the project using a personal computer before subject recruitment." Comment: This describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence, which meets the criterion for truly random sequence generation.

Quote: "eligible selected subjects signed the consent form and completed the baseline measures ... before the counselor opened a serially numbered, opaque, and sealed envelope (SNOSE) to reveal the random assignment of each smoker" and "The random numbers for group assignment were generated by the research assistant (not the counselors) ... before subject recruitment." Comment: This explicitly describes a SNOSE mechanism with all required features (sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed) opened only after enrollment/baseline assessment, which prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a single-blinded randomized controlled trial (RCT)" and "before the counselor opened a serially numbered, opaque, and sealed envelope (SNOSE) to reveal the random assignment of each smoker to A1 or A2 group." Comment: The study explicitly states only single blinding, and participants were assigned to receive either 1 week or 2 weeks of free NRT, so participants and the delivering counselor were likely aware of the allocated NRT duration. Because the outcomes included self-reported smoking cessation and related behavioral outcomes, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence both participant behavior and personnel interactions, making performance bias a high risk overall.

Quote: "An independent interviewer, who was unaware of the subject's group allocation, carried out the 6 and 12 months follow-up interview." and "The main outcome was self-reported 7-day point prevalence quit rate at 6 months"; "At 6 months, those who had stopped smoking ... were invited to attend the SCHC for biochemical validation by measuring carbon monoxide level in expired air." Comment: The primary outcome assessment was done by an independent interviewer blinded to allocation, which reduces detection bias for the self-reported outcomes. The secondary biochemical validation used exhaled carbon monoxide, an objective measure unlikely to be influenced by assessor knowledge. Therefore, outcome assessment is at low risk of bias.

Quote: "We conducted all analyses on an intention-to-treat basis. Follow-up variables with missing data were set to their baseline values (e.g. current smoker)." Comment: The study explicitly used ITT with a conservative missing-data approach, and the results table reports full randomized denominators at 6 and 12 months (A1 n=284; A2 n=278) with no indication of differential loss or imbalance in follow-up. Although the paper notes some participants were lost to follow-up, the overall attrition picture appears reasonable and balanced, and the handling of missing data makes attrition bias unlikely.
